title: Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases
subtitle: The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.
caption:
Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases

The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.

This choropleth map displays the biweekly percentage change in confirmed COVID-19 cases across the globe as of February 22, 2026. The visualization utilizes a diverging color scale to represent the data, where shades of blue indicate a decrease in cases ranging from -100% to 0%, and shades of orange indicate an increase ranging from 0% to 100%. A hatched pattern denotes regions where no data is available. The map shows that the vast majority of the world, including the United States, Canada, Russia, China, India, Australia, and nearly the entire African continent, falls into the "No data" category. Among the countries with reported figures, the distribution is split between declining and rising case numbers. In the Americas, Peru and Guatemala appear in the darkest blue band, representing a biweekly change between -100% and -75%, indicating a sharp reduction in reported cases. Conversely, Brazil and Argentina are shaded in light orange, placing them in the 0% to 25% range, which signifies a slight increase. In Europe, a cluster of nations including Spain, Italy, and Greece sits in the light blue band, corresponding to a change between -25% and 0%. Meanwhile, Sweden and Norway are colored in light orange, showing a rise between 0% and 25%. The data visible on the map highlights a geographic disparity where specific nations in South America and Northern Europe are experiencing growth, while others in South America are seeing significant declines, all amidst a global landscape largely devoid of reported data for this specific metric.
